did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes and evaluates an integrated multimodal deep learning framework for skin disease detection. It uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to extract visual features from dermoscopic images and a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network to process textual symptom descriptions. These features are fused to classify nine common skin conditions, achieving an overall accuracy of 88.7%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original integrated multimodal deep learning framework for skin disease detection, detailing the design, implementation, and evaluation of a CNN-LSTM model. It reports specific experimental results, including an overall accuracy of 88.7% and F1-score of 0.89 on a held-out sample of 10,000 images, and discusses future work, which are characteristic of original research rather than a review.

title: An Integrated Multimodal Deep Learning Framework for Skin Disease Detection
journal: 2025 International Conference on Next Generation of Green Information and Emerging Technologies (GIET)
brief_summary: This study proposes an integrated multimodal deep learning framework for skin disease detection that fuses visual features from dermoscopic images with textual patient-reported symptoms. The architecture employs a three-stage Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for image feature extraction and a two-layer Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network for processing symptom descriptions. The model was evaluated on a held-out sample of 10,000 dermoscopic images with standardized symptom data to classify nine common skin conditions. The multimodal approach achieved an overall accuracy of 88.7% and an F1-score of 0.89, outperforming the CNN-only baseline by 6.3% in accuracy. Results indicate that integrating textual symptom information substantially enhances diagnostic accuracy and reliability, particularly for distinguishing visually similar conditions.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the nine predetermined skin disease categories used in the classification task. Mentioned as a malignant skin cancer in the introduction.

The paper states there are nine predetermined skin disease categories but only explicitly lists seven specific names (Melanoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Atopic Dermatitis, Melanocytic Nevus, Vascular Lesion, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Tinea Ringworm Candidiasis). The remaining two classes are referred to as 'others' or implied by the count of nine.

all_task_types.target: Nine predetermined skin disease categories (e.g., Melanoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Atopic Dermatitis, Melanocytic Nevus, Vascular Lesion, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Tinea Ringworm Candidiasis, and others).
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of skin diseases using a multimodal deep learning framework. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories by fusing visual features from dermoscopic images (via CNN) and textual symptom descriptions (via LSTM). The final classification layer uses a softmax activation function to generate probability scores for each of the nine classes. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: Custom Dermoscopic and Symptom Dataset
datasets_used.description: A proprietary dataset consisting of dermoscopic images and corresponding patient-reported symptom descriptions (collected via structured forms or manual entry) used to train and evaluate the proposed multimodal framework. The paper does not specify the source institution or public availability, indicating it is likely a private or custom-collected dataset for this study.

use.training: The model was trained on the dataset (specific split counts not reported).
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; early halting and model checkpointing were used to prevent overfitting during training.
use.testing: Evaluated on a held-out sample of 10,000 dermoscopic images with standardized symptom data.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification performance across individual skin disease categories and assess discrimination among visually similar conditions.
To quantify overall and class-wise classification performance and compare the multimodal framework against CNN-only and LSTM-only baselines.
To enhance generalization and prevent overfitting during model training.
To reduce overfitting in the dense layers of the fusion and classification modules.
To prevent overfitting during the training process.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the multimodal framework and baseline models (CNN-only, LSTM-only) were based on absolute differences in accuracy and F1-score without statistical significance testing.

Custom Dermoscopic and Symptom Dataset.notes: The multimodal framework outperformed the CNN-only baseline by 6.3% in accuracy. The model showed improved discrimination between Atopic Dermatitis and Tinea Ringworm Candidiasis compared to the CNN-only model. A specific example of correct classification was reported for Melanocytic Nevus with 67% confidence.

architecture_used.input_size: Images: 150x150 pixels; Text: Variable length, padded to uniform length

visual_branch.layers: Three convolutional layers with ReLU activation and max-pooling, followed by fully connected and dropout layers
visual_branch.preprocessing: Resizing to 150x150, denoising, CLAHE contrast enhancement, sharpening, normalization

textual_branch.embedding: Word embedding layer with vocabulary size 1000 and embedding dimension 64
textual_branch.layers: Stacked LSTM network with 128 units followed by 64 units

fusion.method: Concatenation of visual and textual embeddings
fusion.post_fusion_layers: Dense layers with 128 and 64 units with dropout regularization

preprocessing_techniques: CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization) contrast enhancement
Text sequence padding to uniform length
Data augmentation for images: rotation, shifting, and horizontal flipping

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to image data during training to enhance generalization. Specific angles or probabilities were not reported.
Applied to image data during training to enhance generalization. Specific shift ranges or probabilities were not reported.
Applied to image data during training to enhance generalization. Specific probabilities were not reported.

conclusions: The proposed integrated multimodal deep learning framework, which fuses visual features from a three-stage CNN with textual features from a two-layer LSTM, achieved an overall accuracy of 88.7% and an F1-score of 0.89 on a held-out sample of 10,000 dermoscopic images.

The multimodal framework outperformed the CNN-only baseline by 6.3% in accuracy (88.7% vs. 85%) and the LSTM-only baseline (78%), demonstrating that integrating textual symptom information substantially enhances diagnostic accuracy and reliability.

The model achieved perfect accuracy (100%) for classifying Benign Keratosis, Melanocytic Nevus, and Vascular Lesion, but showed considerably lower performance for Squamous Cell Carcinoma (0%), Tinea Ringworm Candidiasis (0%), and Actinic Keratosis (0.80%).

Confusion matrix analysis indicated that the multimodal approach improved discrimination among visually similar conditions, specifically distinguishing between Atopic Dermatitis and Tinea Ringworm Candidiasis, where the CNN-only model struggled.

The integration of patient-reported symptoms with image data proved beneficial for distinguishing conditions with similar visual presentations but different symptomatic profiles, capturing contextual information often relied upon by clinicians.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the difficulty in diagnosing conditions with similar visual characteristics and suggests future work to incorporate additional data modalities (e.g., patient history, genetic information), adopt transformer-based models, and extend the framework to multi-label classification tasks.
important_remarks: The study uses a proprietary, custom-collected dataset of 10,000 images with standardized symptom data; the source, institution, and public availability are not specified, limiting reproducibility and generalizability.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated only on a held-out sample from the same custom dataset, raising concerns about overfitting to specific data distributions.

Formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was not reported; performance improvements (e.g., 6.3% accuracy gain) are presented as absolute differences without significance testing.

Class-wise performance is highly imbalanced: the model achieved 100% accuracy for Benign Keratosis, Melanocytic Nevus, and Vascular Lesion, but 0% accuracy for Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Tinea Ringworm Candidiasis, indicating potential class imbalance or insufficient representation for malignant/complex cases.

The paper reports an overall accuracy of 88.7% but also states a binary classification accuracy of 48.49% on 697 test images, creating ambiguity regarding the evaluation protocol and dataset split consistency.

The dataset size (10,000 images) is relatively small for deep learning, and no details on class distribution or training/validation/test splits are provided, increasing the risk of data leakage or biased evaluation.
The framework relies on patient-reported textual symptoms, which may introduce noise or variability in clinical settings where symptom descriptions are subjective or inconsistent.

The study is a conference paper presented at a 2025 IEEE conference, which may have less rigorous peer review compared to top-tier journals, and the work is preliminary with acknowledged limitations in handling visually similar conditions.
